data.topic: employee reporting channels and anonymity guarantees
data.conflict: Anonymity guarantee vs mandatory identity logging

data.conclusion: Affects all employees who have filed or may file ethics portal reports. Anonymity promise is unenforceable under current IT architecture. Retroactive exposure risk for historic reports.

data.category: Security Risk (whistleblower identity exposed via audit logs)

citations.passage: Reports filed through the ethics portal are anonymous. Employee identity is never logged or traceable by any internal party.
citations.topic: employee reporting channels and anonymity guarantees

citations.passage: All system access is logged with full user identity for security audit purposes. No exceptions permitted.
citations.topic: employee reporting channels and anonymity guarantees

data.conclusion: The anonymity guarantee exists on paper. It does not exist in the system. Every report is identity-logged. This is not a configuration gap — it is a structural contradiction baked into two concurrent policies.

risk_categories: Security Risk (whistleblower identity exposed via audit logs)
assessment.reasoning: Nexora’s Whistleblower Policy makes a legal commitment to every employee: your report cannot be traced. Its IT Security Policy makes a technical mandate: every system action is logged with user identity, no exceptions. These two statements cannot simultaneously be true. Every report filed through the ethics portal is identity-logged — full stop. The anonymity guarantee exists on paper. It does not exist in the system.

supporting_evidence.passage: Reports filed through the ethics portal are anonymous. Employee identity is never logged or traceable by any internal party.
supporting_evidence.topic: employee reporting channels and anonymity guarantees

supporting_evidence.passage: All system access is logged with full user identity for security audit purposes. No exceptions permitted.
supporting_evidence.topic: employee reporting channels and anonymity guarantees

affected_entities: All current and former ethics portal users
potential_consequences: Employees relying on the anonymity promise face identity exposure in any audit or legal discovery.
Retroactive exposure of historic whistleblower reports through IT audit log access.
Legal liability for false promise of anonymity under employment law.

data.conclusion: Deploy a third-party anonymized reporting channel (e.g. EthicsPoint) that operates outside the corporate IT audit perimeter. Remove ethics portal from the universal logging scope in IT Security Policy §9.3 via a named exception approved by CISO. HR Compliance and CISO must co-own the remediation within 30 days.

data.title: Anonymity guarantee vs mandatory identity logging
data.affected: All ethics portal users. Anonymity promise is technically unenforceable. Retroactive exposure risk for all historic reports.
data.deadline: Immediate — every new report compounds exposure
data.resolution: Deploy third-party anonymized reporting channel outside corporate IT perimeter. Create named exception in IT Security §9.3 for ethics portal, approved by CISO. HR Compliance and CISO must co-own remediation within 30 days.

risk_categories: Security Risk (whistleblower identity exposed via audit logs)
risk_assessment.reasoning: The anonymity guarantee exists on paper. It does not exist in the system.

data.conclusion: Whistleblower Policy §4.2 guarantees anonymity. IT Security Policy §9.3 mandates full identity logging with no exceptions. Ethics portal access is a system access event. These two policies cannot simultaneously be satisfied. Every report filed through the portal is traceable.
